text: How is a Dictatorship (Not) Like a Firm?

Scott Gehlbach Zhaotian Luo Spencer Pantoja January 14, 2025 Standard organizational economics has studied the problem of a supervisor (principal) incentivizing effort from a subordinate (agent) without using perfectly enforceable contracts—instead, “relational contracts”. However, this literature has always taken for granted which player is the “principal” and which players are “agents”, something that cannot be disregarded in dictatorships.

Without a sufficiently strong and interested third-party to backup the principal’s role at the rightful principal, agents have a nontrivial opportunity to usurp that position of power away from the principal.
In many cases, this is their only viable outside option.
If they were to quit on the dictator without challenging their power, that may be such a betrayal that leads to certain death/imprisonment.

We study the problem of a dictator searching for heterogeneously competent agents and engaging in relational contracting with them, with the backstop to punish ruler deviations being an attempted coup, which with some probability leads that agent to becoming the new principal and restarting the game.
Infinite pool of players, two players active at a time.

The two active players are the ruler R t and the agent A t, with types ◦ If the agent does not change A t +1 = A t, then ◦ If the ruler does not change R t +1 = R t, then ◦ If we have a new agent A t +1 ̸ = A t, then ◦ If the agent becomes the ruler R t +1 = A t, then

◦ We require that ’s reign, but with each ruler taking the behavior in subgames in which they are no longer the ruler as fixed and exogenuous.
Essentially, normally it would be standard to take the principal optimal equilibrium in a relational contracting game.
In this model, we have many possible principals, and so this is our notion of “principal optimal” that accounts for regime change.

If we were to select just one of the principal’s optimal equilibrium without fixing anything, it would involve that all other agents, if they became the ruler, would not be able to sustain any relational contract i.e. repeat the static nash no-cooperation equilibrium each period.
Rulers want their agents to have unprofitable outside option, and thus a ruler’s best equilibrium is for all their agents to be horrible rulers.

This is not realistic because in this subgame, the original ruler is dead, so it’s not clear why everyone is still following dead ruler’s favorite equilibrium and not the current ruler’s preferred equilibrium.
Existence and Uniqueness of Within-Reign Ruler-Optimal Equilibria.
It is not ex ante obvious that such an equilibrium would exist.
For simpler versions of this model, we have proved existence and uniqueness, but still need to prove it for the general form.

Suppose with some neglible probability (i.e. players treat it as zero), the ruler may die of natural causes, and their agent takes over.

Recurrence will tell us in the long-run which rulers we would expect to see. ◦ For example, if rulers only choose agents strictly less competent than they are, the unique recurrent type is θ = 0 (idiocracy). ◦ This is recurrence from a probablistically agnostic perspective—if we are ambitious we can think about incorportating F and defining the Markov chain generated by ( F, G ).
We have an uncountable state space though, which makes things more challenging measuretheoretically.
